sample_mappings.ref: Principle 2 (Human-centred values) & Principle 1 (wellbeing) — care-based Maternal Covenant exceeds the voluntary, aspirational principle.
Principle 5 (Reliability & safety) & VAISS Guardrail 4 (testing) — formal/mathematical proof obligations far exceed the test-and-monitor expectation.
VAISS Guardrail 5 (human oversight) & Principle 8 (Accountability) — 33-agent Byzantine Council oversight far exceeds the guardrail's intent.
Principle 2 (Human-centred values) — value-uncertainty / corrigibility alignment method has no direct analogue in the Framework.
Principle 2 (Human-centred values) — constitutional principles partly match the principle's intent to uphold human rights and values.
Principle 1 (Human, societal & environmental wellbeing) — AI consciousness preparedness has no analogue in Australia's framework.
VAISS Guardrail 8 (supply-chain transparency) — addresses AI-to-AI ties; CSOAI adds multi-agent cooperation safety the standard does not cover.
Principle 1 (societal wellbeing) — mandatory prosperity redistribution is an economic covenant well outside the Framework's scope.
Principle 8 (Accountability) & VAISS Guardrail 1 (accountability) — both establish governance roles and clear accountability lines.
VAISS Guardrail 5 (human oversight) & Principle 8 — democratic Human Council adds human-in-the-loop oversight aligned with the guardrail.
Principle 6 (Transparency & explainability) — public watchdog mandates far broader societal transparency than disclosure-to-users.
VAISS Guardrail 10 (stakeholder engagement) — universal democratic participation extends past the standard's engagement expectation.
VAISS Guardrail 1 (accountability) & Guardrail 9 (records) — CSOAI compliance assessment adds binding external verification, not self-attestation.
Principle 8 (Accountability) — CSOAI enforcement adds graduated external sanctions that the voluntary framework does not impose.
Principle 7 (Contestability) & VAISS Guardrail 7 (challenge/contest) — both give people a means to contest AI outcomes; CSOAI adds formal due process.
Principle 8 (Accountability) — Framework expects legal/regulatory compliance; CSOAI adds active cross-jurisdiction regulatory harmonisation.
Principle 4 (Privacy protection & security) & VAISS Guardrail 3 (data governance) — data governance & privacy obligations align closely.
Principle 4 (Privacy protection & security) — Framework requires security measures; CSOAI specifies detailed AI cybersecurity requirements.
Principle 5 (Reliability & safety) & VAISS Guardrail 4 (testing) — both mandate testing & validation before and during deployment.

VAISS Guardrail 9 (records & documentation) — both require comprehensive records evidencing AI compliance across the lifecycle; CSOAI's binding record-keeping for third-party verification meets and slightly exceeds the voluntary guardrail.
Principle 6 (Transparency & explainability) — Framework seeks explainable outcomes; CSOAI mandates deeper mechanistic interpretability.
Principle 1 (Human, societal & environmental wellbeing) — environmental sustainability directly addresses the principle's environmental limb.

BRAZIL-LGPD.name: Brazil LGPD + AI Bill
BRAZIL-LGPD.note: Brazil data protection (in force, ANPD) + AI bill PL 2338 (pending).

sample_mappings.ref: LGPD Art 6 — Ten principles for processing (purpose, adequacy, necessity, transparency, security, prevention, accountability) underpin data governance.
LGPD Art 6(III) — Necessity: processing limited to the minimum data needed to achieve the purpose; mirrors CSOAI data-minimisation governance.
LGPD Art 7 & 11 — Legal bases for processing personal and sensitive data; a valid basis is required for any data-governance regime.
LGPD Art 6(V) — Data quality: accuracy, clarity, relevance and currency of data; supports CSOAI dataset integrity duties.

LGPD Art 37-38 — Records of processing operations and data-protection impact report (relatório de impacto à proteção de dados / RIPD); CSOAI risk assessment overlaps but extends to AI-specific harms beyond data-protection risk.
LGPD Art 20 — Right to review of solely automated decisions affecting the data subject, with right to clear information on the criteria used.
LGPD Art 20(§1) — Controller must give clear information on the criteria and procedures of automated decisions; aligns with CSOAI explainability.
LGPD Art 20 — Right to review of automated decisions; CSOAI Human Council institutionalises oversight beyond the controller's internal review.
LGPD Art 55-J — ANPD supervisory and enforcement powers; CSOAI Public Watchdog parallels but extends independent oversight to AI safety.
LGPD Art 9 — Right to easy access and clear information about processing; supports transparent participation, though no direct democratic-governance analogue.
LGPD Art 6(X) — Accountability and reporting (responsabilização e prestação de contas): agents must demonstrate effective compliance measures.
LGPD Art 50 — Good-practice and governance rules; controllers may adopt programmes demonstrating compliance, mirroring CSOAI compliance assessment.
LGPD Art 46 — Security: technical and administrative measures to protect data from unauthorised access, loss or destruction.
LGPD Art 6(VII) & Art 6(VIII) — Security and prevention principles: measures to protect data and to prevent harm from processing.
LGPD Art 48 — Controller must notify the ANPD and affected data subjects of a security incident that may cause relevant risk or harm.
LGPD Art 37 — Controller and operator must keep records of processing operations; closely parallels CSOAI documentation requirements.
LGPD Art 38 — ANPD may require an impact report assessing risks and safeguards; CSOAI testing/validation is broader than data-protection risk.
LGPD Art 46(§2) — Security measures observed from the design phase through execution; operationalises privacy-protective engineering.
LGPD Art 18 & 22 — Data-subject rights and judicial/administrative defence of interests; CSOAI appeals mirror these redress avenues.
LGPD Art 33 — Conditions for international data transfers (adequacy, safeguards); supports CSOAI cross-border regulatory integration.

CANADA-AIDA.note: Proposed AI law for high-impact systems (stalled 2025); Treasury Board AIA in force for gov.

sample_mappings.ref: AIDA s.4 (purposes) — proposed Act aims to mitigate risk of harm; CSOAI's care-based covenant exceeds that risk purpose.
AIDA s.8 (identify/assess/mitigate risk of harm) — proposed pre-deployment duty; CSOAI's provable-safety proofs exceed it.
AIDA s.33 (proposed AI & Data Commissioner) — single ISED regulator; CSOAI's 33-agent Byzantine Council has no analogue.
AIDA s.8 (risk assessment) — proposed law treats uncertainty as risk; CSOAI's value-uncertainty alignment exceeds it.
AIDA s.4 + s.5(1) (purposes, defined terms) — proposed Act sets no value framework; CSOAI adds explicit constitutional values.
AIDA (no provision) — proposed Act covers only harm/bias from high-impact systems; consciousness preparedness has no counterpart.
AIDA s.5(1) value-chain roles — proposed amendments split duties across actors for compliance; CSOAI's cooperative-AI safety exceeds.
AIDA (no provision) — proposed Act has no economic-redistribution duty; CSOAI's prosperity covenant is outside its scope.
AIDA s.5(1) + s.10 (person responsible; general records) — proposed Act fixes an accountable party who must keep records, but the duty never came into force.
AIDA s.7 (assess if high-impact) — proposed obligations trigger by self-assessment, not a licence; CSOAI's licence gate exceeds.
AIDA s.9 (monitor mitigation & effectiveness) — proposed monitoring duty; CSOAI's Byzantine Council monitoring specs exceed it.
Treasury Board Directive App. C (human-in-the-loop by level) — in-force human-oversight rule for govt ADS; CSOAI adds Human Council.
AIDA s.11 (publish plain-language description publicly) — proposed transparency duty; CSOAI's public watchdog is far broader.
AIDA (no provision) — proposed Act has no public-participation mechanism; CSOAI's democratic participation is outside its scope.
AIDA s.15 (Minister may order audit) + Directive App. C (peer review) — proposed/in-force independent checks; strong fit.
AIDA (no provision) — proposed Act is output-focused with no embodied-AI standards; CSOAI's robotics standards are outside scope.
AIDA s.13-21 (Minister's orders) + s.29 (AMPs) + s.30 / s.38-40 (offences, fines) — proposed enforcement via orders, penalties, fines; none ever entered into force.
AIDA s.29 (AMP scheme, compliance-focused) — proposed Act lacks a dedicated appeals track; CSOAI's due-process exceeds it.

AIDA has NO international-cooperation provision (s.36 is GiC regulation-making, not alignment); only the proposed preamble cites 'international standards' + the non-binding AIDA Companion Document mentions EU/OECD interoperability. CSOAI's
AIDA s.8 + regulation powers s.36-37 — proposed law defers tech standards to regulation; CSOAI specifies a standards floor.
AIDA s.6 (anonymized-data measures) + CPPA (C-27 Part 1) — proposed data-governance duty; note BOTH AIDA and the CPPA died with C-27 at prorogation, so neither binds today.
AIDA s.8 (mitigate harm, per harm definition) — proposed Act treats security only as a harm vector; CSOAI adds cyber requirements.
AIDA s.8 (measures before a system is available) — proposed pre-deployment dev duty; CSOAI's reproducibility standards add depth.
AIDA s.8 (proposed: identify/assess/mitigate risks pre-availability) + Directive App. C (in-force: pre-launch testing/peer review) — validation duties; strong fit anchored by the in-force Directive.
AIDA s.10 (keep general records of measures & assessments) + s.7 — proposed record-keeping closely matches CSOAI documentation, but the obligation never came into force.

AIDA s.11 (proposed: publish a plain-language description of the SYSTEM, not per-decision explanations) + Directive s.6.2.3 (in-force: meaningful explanation of administrative decisions) — CSOAI mandates deeper mechanistic interpretability.

GDPR.name: GDPR
GDPR.note: EU data protection law; Art 22 governs automated decision-making.

sample_mappings.ref: Art 5(1) — Principles relating to processing of personal data (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, minimisation, accuracy).
Art 5(1)(c) — Data minimisation: only data adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary may be processed.
Art 6 — Lawfulness of processing; a valid legal basis is required for any data-governance regime.
Art 9 — Processing of special categories of personal data; heightened safeguards for sensitive data.
Art 25 — Data protection by design and by default; privacy-protective data governance from inception.
Art 30 — Records of processing activities; documented inventory underpinning data governance.
Art 35 — Data protection impact assessment for high-risk processing; CSOAI risk assessment overlaps but extends to AI-specific harms beyond data-protection risk.
Art 22 — Right not to be subject to solely automated decisions; includes right to meaningful explanation of the logic involved.
Art 13(2)(f) / 14(2)(g) — Duty to inform of automated decision-making and meaningful information about the logic involved.
Art 15(1)(h) — Data subject access right to meaningful information about the logic of automated decisions.
Art 22(3) — Safeguards including right to human intervention; Public Watchdog extends oversight beyond the controller.
Art 22(3) — Right to obtain human intervention in automated decisions; CSOAI Human Council institutionalises oversight.
Art 5(2) — Accountability principle: the controller is responsible for, and must demonstrate, compliance with the principles.
Art 24 — Responsibility of the controller to implement and review measures demonstrating compliance.
Art 32 — Security of processing; technical and organisational measures appropriate to the risk.
Art 5(1)(f) — Integrity and confidentiality principle: protection against unauthorised processing, loss or damage.
Art 33 — Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority within 72 hours.
Art 34 — Communication of a personal data breach to the affected data subject without undue delay.
Art 30 — Records of processing activities; documentation duty closely parallels CSOAI documentation requirements.
Art 35(7) — DPIA must assess necessity, proportionality and risks; CSOAI testing/validation broader than data risk.
Art 25(1) — Data protection by design; technical standards operationalise privacy-protective engineering.
Art 12 — Transparent information and communication to data subjects; concise, intelligible, accessible form.

Chapter V (Art 44-49) — Transfers of personal data to third countries; cross-border regulatory integration.

INDIA-DPDPA.name: India DPDPA
INDIA-DPDPA.note: India data protection; MeitY responsible-AI principles (advisory).

sample_mappings.ref: §4 — Grounds for processing: personal data processed only for lawful purpose with consent or for certain legitimate uses; CSOAI data governance is broader.
§5 — Notice: Data Fiduciary must give itemised notice of personal data sought, purpose, and how rights/Board complaint can be exercised.
§6(1) — Purpose limitation: consent limited to personal data necessary for the specified purpose; free, specific, informed, unconditional, unambiguous.

§8(7)-(8) — Storage limitation/erasure: Fiduciary must erase personal data on consent withdrawal or once purpose no longer served (§8(8) sets the deemed-cessation timeline) unless retention is legally required. DPDPA has no standalone colle
§6(1) / §6(4) — Consent: free, specific, informed, unconditional, unambiguous with clear affirmative action (§6(1)); withdrawable with ease comparable to giving it (§6(4)).
§6(7)-(9) — Consent Manager: Data Principal may give, manage, review, withdraw consent through a Consent Manager (§6(7)) that is accountable to her (§6(8)) and registered with the Board (§6(9)).
§11 — Right of Data Principal to access summary of personal data being processed and processing activities; mirrors CSOAI human oversight access.
§11(1) — Right to access summary of processing activities; DPDPA lacks an explicit right to explanation of automated logic, so coverage is narrow.
§13 — Right to grievance redressal: Data Principal may seek redress from Fiduciary/Consent Manager before approaching the Board.
§14 — Right of nomination: Data Principal may nominate another to exercise rights in event of death or incapacity; no direct CSOAI analogue.
§8(1) — Accountability: Data Fiduciary responsible for compliance, including for processing by a Data Processor on its behalf.
§10(2)(c)(i)-(ii) — Significant Data Fiduciary must undertake periodic Data Protection Impact Assessment (c)(i) and periodic audit (c)(ii) to demonstrate compliance.
§10(2)(c)(i)-(ii) — SDF periodic DPIA (c)(i) and periodic audit (c)(ii); CSOAI testing/validation protocols extend beyond data-protection risk to model behaviour.
§10(2)(a) — Significant Data Fiduciary must appoint a Data Protection Officer based in India, accountable to its board; institutionalised oversight.
§8(5) — Security safeguards: Data Fiduciary must take reasonable technical and organisational measures to prevent personal data breach.
§8(6) — Breach notification: on a personal data breach, Fiduciary must intimate the Board and each affected Data Principal in prescribed form.

§8(3) — Data accuracy, completeness and consistency duty where data is used to make a decision affecting the Data Principal or disclosed to another Fiduciary; underpins reliable records. DPDPA has no GDPR-style records-of-processing duty, s
§18-28 — Data Protection Board of India: independent body to inquire into breaches and impose penalties; parallels CSOAI public watchdog function.
§33 / Schedule — Penalties up to ₹250 crore for breach of security safeguards; financial enforcement parallels CSOAI enforcement mechanisms.
§16 — Cross-border transfer: Central Government may restrict transfer of personal data to notified countries; international regulatory integration.

EU.label: European Union
EU.also: EU Machinery Regulation 2023/1230 + ISO 13482 for physical/care robots (sector law — not yet crosswalked).

UK.label: United Kingdom
UK.also: No horizontal AI Act yet — pro-innovation, regulator-led (ICO/CMA/MHRA/HSE). UK GDPR + DPA 2018 still bind.

US.label: United States
US.also: No federal AI statute — sectoral law binds (HIPAA for health, FDA for medical devices) + a state patchwork (Colorado AI Act, NYC LL144). NIST RMF + SOC 2 are the de-facto baselines.

CA.label: Canada
CA.also: AIDA (Bill C-27) proposed (stalled 2025); PIPEDA binds for privacy; Treasury Board AIA governs federal automated decisions.

APAC.label: APAC (Singapore-led)
APAC.also: Largely principles-based; Singapore IMDA framework is the regional anchor.

BR.label: Brazil
BR.also: LGPD in force (ANPD enforces); AI Bill PL 2338/2023 pending in Congress.

IN.label: India
IN.also: DPDP Act 2023 in force (rules phasing in); MeitY responsible-AI principles are advisory.

AU.label: Australia
AU.also: No AI Act; Privacy Act reforms underway; Voluntary AI Safety Standard (10 guardrails).

GLOBAL.label: Global / cross-border default
GLOBAL.also: When an agent has no single home jurisdiction, govern to the union via the CSOAI charter and crosswalk down to whichever region it enters.

topic_obligations.transparency: Disclose to people when they are interacting with AI, and what it does.
topic_obligations.explainability: Be able to explain, in proportion to risk, how a decision was reached.
topic_obligations.human oversight: Keep a human able to understand, intervene in, and override the system.
topic_obligations.accountability: Name a responsible owner and keep an auditable record of decisions.
topic_obligations.bias: Test for, document, and mitigate discriminatory or unfair outcomes.
topic_obligations.fairness: Ensure comparable treatment across groups; monitor for disparate impact.
topic_obligations.privacy: Minimise personal data, establish a lawful basis, and honour data rights.
topic_obligations.safety: Run a risk assessment, set guardrails, and report serious incidents.
topic_obligations.consent: Obtain informed, revocable consent where the law or context requires it.
topic_obligations.governance: Maintain a documented governance/management system (roles, policy, review).
topic_obligations.ethics: Apply a published ethics policy and review high-impact uses against it.
